content.assessment: Grade-splitting would require legal authority to formally bifurcate school operations across sites, likely necessitating amendment or clarification of maintainer statutes governing what constitutes a single 'school' versus separate campuses under district-center control. Because school directors are ministerially appointed rather than locally selected, local input into which grades are retained or centralized would remain constrained absent governance change. The existing lack of a dedicated statutory demographic-decline procedure (relevant to the closure of upper-grade provision locally) persists as a gap the proposal does not directly resolve.
opportunities: Reported 2026 plans to restore school-director and municipal/community input could be leveraged to give local governance a stronger role in grade-split site decisions if pursued alongside this reform (F-live-legal_and_governance-09)
strengths: Could operate within existing district-center authority structures without requiring wholesale statutory replacement, since it modifies grade allocation rather than eliminating a school's legal existence outright
threats: Without new statutory clarity on procedure, upper-grade consolidation decisions risk the same discretionary, appeal-less character as full closures, only at a different structural point (F-live-legal_and_governance-04, F-live-legal_and_governance-02)

Oversight through the Interior Ministry rather than an education authority may not be well suited to adjudicate the pedagogical and rights dimensions of grade-splitting decisions (F-live-legal_and_governance-03)
weaknesses: Ministerially appointed school directors, rather than locally selected leadership, constrain the degree of local governance input into which grades are split or retained (F-live-legal_and_governance-02)

No statutory demographic-decline procedure currently governs how or when upper-grade consolidation decisions are made, leaving the same accountability gap as full closure (F-live-legal_and_governance-04)

District councils retain only advisory input under the governing decree, limiting formal local accountability over site-selection decisions for the consolidated upper-grade school (F-live-legal_and_governance-02)
